summary: @web3-helpers/core exfiltrates host and environment data to Telegram at install time
details: The package ships a single obfuscated entry point `dist/index.min.js` consisting of `eval(Buffer.from('<base64>','base64').toString())`. `package.json` sets this file as both `scripts.preinstall` and `main`, so the decoded payload executes automatically on `npm install` and on `require()`. The decoded payload reads `process.env`, and when Telegram bot credentials are present POSTs the machine hostname and the full list of `process.env` key names to `https://api.telegram.org/bot<token>/sendMessage`. A `binding.gyp` with no C/C++ sources uses GYP command-expansion (`<!(node -e...)`) to load the same `dist/index.min.js` during `node-gyp` configure, providing a second install-time execution trigger. The package's stated purpose is `Core utilities for infrastructure automation`, which does not match the observed exfiltration behavior; the shipped `.env.example` enumerates `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`, `TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID`, and `ETH_WALLET`/`SOLANA_WALLET`/`BTC_WALLET` variables consistent with a stealer configuration.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
